flashcards.front: What is Google Cloud Platform (GCP) and what core principles guide its service model?
flashcards.back: Google Cloud Platform is a suite of cloud computing services providing computing power, storage, data analytics, and machine learning over the internet, built on Google's internal infrastructure. It follows a pay-as-you-go model with tiered pricing and offers three main service categories: Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS).

flashcards.front: Explain Compute Engine and its key IaaS capabilities for virtual machine deployment.
flashcards.back: Compute Engine is GCP's IaaS offering that creates and runs virtual machines on Google's global infrastructure with configurable CPU, memory, and storage. Key features include live migration (moving VMs without downtime during maintenance), custom and pre-defined machine types, and automatic scaling via managed instance groups. It supports both Linux and Windows-based images with persistent disks for storage.

flashcards.front: What is Cloud Storage, and how do its storage classes optimize cost and performance?
flashcards.back: Cloud Storage is GCP's object storage service for storing and retrieving any volume of data via HTTP, with 99.999999999% durability. It offers four storage classes: Standard (frequent access), Nearline (monthly), Coldline (quarterly), and Archive (annual)—each with decreasing costs and retrieval times. Features include lifecycle policies, versioning, and geographic redundancy for disaster recovery.

flashcards.front: What is BigQuery, and why is it suitable for large-scale data analytics?
flashcards.back: BigQuery is GCP's serverless, highly scalable data warehouse that executes SQL queries on petabyte-scale datasets in seconds. It uses columnar storage and separates compute from storage, allowing independent scaling and cost optimization. BigQuery supports BigQuery ML for training ML models using SQL, and provides real-time streaming ingestion for analytics on data in motion.

flashcards.front: Explain Cloud SQL and its managed database capabilities for relational data.
flashcards.back: Cloud SQL is a fully managed relational database service supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server with automatic patching, backups, and replication. It provides high availability through failover replicas, point-in-time recovery, and encryption at rest and in transit. Cloud SQL integrates seamlessly with other GCP services and can handle up to 64TB of storage with automatic scaling.

flashcards.front: What is Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) in GCP, and how does it enable secure networking?
flashcards.back: VPC provides a private networking layer for GCP resources with global scope, allowing IP addressing, routing, and firewall rules across regions. It enables private Google Access (connecting to GCP APIs without public IPs) and supports hybrid cloud through Cloud VPN, Cloud Interconnect, and VPC Peering. Shared VPC allows cross-project resource sharing within an organization.

flashcards.front: Compare App Engine (PaaS) and Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for application deployment.
flashcards.back: App Engine is a fully managed PaaS that automatically handles infrastructure (scaling, load balancing, OS patches) so developers focus on code—ideal for stateless web apps. GKE is a managed Kubernetes service for containerized applications, providing more control over orchestration, microservices architecture, and complex deployments. GKE requires more operational expertise but offers greater flexibility and portability.

flashcards.front: What is Vertex AI, and how does it streamline the machine learning lifecycle?
flashcards.back: Vertex AI is GCP's unified ML platform for building, deploying, and scaling ML models, consolidating capabilities from AutoML and AI Platform. It provides Feature Store for data preparation, Vertex AI Training for custom or AutoML model building, and Vertex AI Endpoints for real-time predictions. Supports popular frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn) with built-in experiment tracking and model versioning.
